---
title: "Multi-Channel Storage & Cross-Channel Transforms"
---

## Motivation

Multi-channel acquisition data (e.g. 32–384 channels from an analog-to-digital
converter) is a core data type in neural recording workflows. Today these channels
are represented as independent `AnalogTimeSeries` objects with no awareness of each
other. This creates two categories of problems:

1. **No cross-channel operations.** Common Average Referencing (CAR), spatial
   whitening, and other cross-channel transforms require simultaneous access to all
   channels at a given time point. Independent `AnalogTimeSeries` objects have no
   mechanism for this.

2. **Redundant I/O for memory-mapped data.** When channels are stored in an
   interleaved binary file (32 int16 values for $t_1$, 32 int16 values for $t_2$, …),
   each channel's `MemoryMappedAnalogDataStorage` reads the same file pages via
   stride/offset but discards the neighboring channel data it had to read anyway.
   For 384 channels, each OS page is effectively read 384 times.

Additionally, the current mmap storage has no block caching:
`getSpanRangeImpl()` always returns an empty span, forcing all access through
element-by-element virtual dispatch with per-element stride calculation, `memcpy`,
type conversion, and scaling.

This document describes a phased refactoring plan to address both problems.
See [GitHub Issue #240](https://github.com/paulmthompson/WhiskerToolbox/issues/240)
for the original feature request.

## Design Principles

- **TensorData is the canonical owner** for multi-channel data.
  `AnalogTimeSeries` objects become lightweight column views.
- **Block cache is file-level**, shared across all channels reading the same file.
- **LibTorch is the compute backend** for cross-channel transforms. Armadillo
  remains the default *storage* backend for `TensorData` and provides zero-copy
  column access for `AnalogTimeSeries` views.
- **GPU acceleration is opt-in** via device selection; the same LibTorch code
  runs on CPU or GPU.
- **Phases are independently shippable.** Each phase produces working, tested code.

## Compute Backend Decision: Armadillo vs LibTorch {#sec-compute-backend}

Cross-channel transforms (CAR, whitening, and future operations) use **LibTorch
as the single compute backend**, even on CPU-only systems. LibTorch is a required
dependency regardless of GPU availability.

### Rationale

| Concern | Armadillo | LibTorch |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| CPU/GPU unification | Separate code paths | Same code, change `torch::Device` |
| Memory layout | Column-major | Row-major default (natural for row-wise cross-channel ops like CAR) |
| Median/mean | `arma::median(mat, 1)` | `torch::median(t, 1)` |
| Eigendecomposition | `arma::eig_sym` | `torch::linalg::eigh` |
| BLAS | System OpenBLAS/LAPACK | MKL (Intel) or highly-tuned OpenBLAS |
| Bulk type conversion | Manual loop | `tensor.to(torch::kFloat32)` — fused, SIMD-friendly |

: Armadillo vs LibTorch comparison for cross-channel compute {.striped}

Maintaining two implementations (Armadillo for CPU, LibTorch for GPU) doubles the
code surface, doubles the test surface, and creates a source of subtle divergence.
With LibTorch, the `use_gpu` parameter reduces to device selection rather than an
entirely different algorithm:

```cpp
auto t = tensor.toLibTorch();
if (use_gpu) {
    t = DeviceManager::instance().toDevice(t);
}
auto ref = torch::median(t, /*dim=*/1).values;
t = t - ref.unsqueeze(1);
```

### Where Armadillo remains

Armadillo continues to serve as the default **storage** backend for `TensorData`
(`ArmadilloTensorStorage`). Its column-major layout is ideal for
`TensorColumnAnalogStorage` (Phase 3) because each column is contiguous —
perfect for `AnalogTimeSeries` span access. The `toLibTorch()` conversion
bridges storage to compute when transforms are applied.

## Storage Layout & Conversion Costs {#sec-storage-layout}

A 32-channel, 1M-sample recording stored in-memory as a `TensorData` lives in
an Armadillo `arma::fmat(1000000, 32)` — column-major. Each column is one
channel, contiguous in memory. $N$ `AnalogTimeSeries` objects (one per channel)
reference this same matrix through `TensorColumnAnalogStorage`, which hands
DataViewer a zero-copy `std::span<float const>` via `fmat.colptr(c)`.

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          arma::fmat  (column-major in memory)        │
│                                                      │
│  col 0 (ch0)  col 1 (ch1)  col 2 (ch2)  …  col 31  │
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐     ┌──────┐ │
│  │ t0      │  │ t0      │  │ t0      │     │ t0   │ │
│  │ t1      │  │ t1      │  │ t1      │     │ t1   │ │
│  │ …       │  │ …       │  │ …       │     │ …    │ │
│  │ t999999 │  │ t999999 │  │ t999999 │     │ …    │ │
│  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘     └──────┘ │
│  ↑ contiguous  ↑ contiguous  ↑ contiguous            │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │               │               │
        ▼               ▼               ▼
  AnalogTimeSeries  AnalogTimeSeries  AnalogTimeSeries
  (TensorColumn     (TensorColumn     (TensorColumn
   Storage, ch0)     Storage, ch1)     Storage, ch2)
```

This layout is the right canonical format for multi-channel data that fits in
memory. Each `AnalogTimeSeries` is a lightweight view — no copies, no
duplication, full compatibility with `DataViewer_Widget` and existing
single-channel infrastructure.

### Why not one flat AnalogTimeSeries?

An alternative would be concatenating all channels end-to-end in a single
`AnalogTimeSeries` (`[ch0_all_t, ch1_all_t, ...]`). This is essentially
what Armadillo column-major memory already is — a contiguous buffer of
$N_{\text{channels}} \times N_{\text{samples}}$ floats. But the matrix
representation is strictly better: it carries shape, column names,
`TimeFrame` metadata, and 2D indexing. Cross-channel transforms index
`mat.col(c)` instead of manually computing `offset + c * num_samples`.
And each `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` view is a first-class
`AnalogTimeSeries` that plugs into DataViewer, transforms, and export
without special-casing.

### Conversion costs: Armadillo ↔ LibTorch

When a cross-channel transform runs, data must move from Armadillo (storage)
to LibTorch (compute) and back. The current `toLibTorch()` implementation
is expensive: it calls `materializeFlat()` which iterates element-by-element
with virtual dispatch per element, converting column-major → row-major.
For 32 channels × 1M samples this is 32M virtual calls plus two full copies.

This is fixable. `torch::from_blob` accepts custom strides, enabling a
**zero-copy view** of Armadillo's column-major memory:

```cpp
auto const & mat = arma_storage->matrix();
auto t = torch::from_blob(
    const_cast<float*>(mat.memptr()),
    {static_cast<int64_t>(mat.n_rows), static_cast<int64_t>(mat.n_cols)},
    {1, static_cast<int64_t>(mat.n_rows)},  // column-major strides
    torch::kFloat32
);
// t is a zero-copy strided view — no data movement
```

LibTorch BLAS operations (`torch::mm`, `torch::median`, `torch::linalg::eigh`)
are all stride-aware. The whitening transform can operate directly on
Armadillo's memory without touching it.

::: {.callout-note}
**Completed (Phase 0):** `TensorData::toLibTorch()` now uses `torch::from_blob`
with column-major strides when the source is `ArmadilloTensorStorage`, followed
by `.contiguous()` to produce a row-major tensor that owns its own memory. This
eliminates the $O(n)$ virtual calls from `materializeFlat()` and reduces the
conversion to a single contiguous memcpy.
:::

With the stride fix, the full round-trip for applying a transform looks like:

| Step | Operation | Copies |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Storage | `arma::fmat(time, channels)` — column-major | canonical |
| View | $N$ `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` views → DataViewer | 0 |
| To compute | `toLibTorch()` → strided view of Armadillo memory | 0 |
| Transform | e.g. whitening — allocates result tensor (row-major) | 1 (result only) |
| Back to storage | `result.toArmadillo()` → new `arma::fmat` (col-major) | 1 (layout convert) |
| New views | $N$ new `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` on result | 0 |

: Round-trip cost for applying a cross-channel transform {.striped}

Total: **one copy for the transform input (zero-copy via strides), one copy
for the output (layout conversion)**. For 128 MB of data (32ch × 1M × 4B),
the output copy is ~30ms — negligible compared to the eigendecomposition in
whitening.

## Transform Responsibility: AnalogTimeSeries vs TensorData {#sec-transform-responsibility}

A key architectural question arises when both single-channel and multi-channel
data exist in the same system: where should per-channel transforms (like IIR
filtering) live?

**Principle:** Single-channel transforms stay on `AnalogTimeSeries`. Multi-channel
transforms — including "apply the same filter to all channels" — go on
`TensorData`.

| Data origin | Transform target | Why |
|-------------|-----------------|-----|
| Single-channel source (e.g. one sensor) | `AnalogTimeSeries` | Data is naturally a contiguous `std::vector<float>`. Existing filter code runs at full speed on a tight span. |
| Multi-channel source (e.g. 32-ch ADC) | `TensorData` | Data lives in a matrix. Per-column iteration on Armadillo columns is contiguous and fast. Cross-channel ops (CAR) need the full matrix. |

: Transform responsibility by data origin {.striped}

The block cache's job (Phase 4) is **efficient I/O and reasonable viewing
performance**, not hot-loop compute. Scrolling through a channel in a
`DataViewer` widget fetches a screen's worth of samples — not a tight
filter loop over millions of elements. Heavy compute (filtering, CAR,
whitening) happens after materializing into a proper `TensorData` where
columns are contiguous and LibTorch can operate on the full matrix.

This avoids a problematic design where single-channel transforms need to
be aware of their storage backend's memory layout. The `AnalogTimeSeries`
filter transform remains fast for its natural use case and does not need
to handle block-cached or strided storage.

## Architecture Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Interleaved Binary File                       │
│  [ch0 ch1 … ch31] [ch0 ch1 … ch31] [ch0 ch1 … ch31] …        │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │ mmap
              ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
              │   SharedMmapBlockCache   │  (Phase 4)
              │   Circular buffer of     │
              │   decoded float blocks   │
              └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     ┌─────┴──────┐
                     ▼            ▼
        ┌────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐
        │  MmapTensor    │  │ BlockCachedMmap │
        │  Storage       │  │ AnalogStorage   │
        │  (TensorData)  │  │ (per-channel)   │
        └───────┬────────┘  └─────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
        ┌───────────────┐     AnalogTimeSeries::
        │  TensorData   │────▶ createFromTensorColumn()
        │  [time × ch]  │     (Phase 3: zero-copy views)
        └───────┬───────┘
                │  Transforms (Phase 2)
                ▼
        ┌───────────────┐
        │  CAR / Whiten │──── LibTorch (CPU or GPU via DeviceManager)
        └───────────────┘
```

## Phase 1: AnalogToTensor & TensorToAnalog Transforms {#sec-phase1}

::: {.callout-note}
**Completed.** Both transforms are implemented, registered, and tested.
See [developer docs](../transforms_v2/analog_to_tensor.qmd) for full API reference.
:::

*No dependencies. Directly addresses Issue #240.*

This phase creates the bridge between independent `AnalogTimeSeries` channels and
a unified `TensorData` representation suitable for cross-channel operations.

### Step 1.1 — AnalogToTensor Container Transform ✓

**Files:**

- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/AnalogToTensor/AnalogToTensor.hpp`
- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/AnalogToTensor/AnalogToTensor.cpp`

**Transform type:** N-ary container (N `AnalogTimeSeries` → 1 `TensorData`).

**Parameters struct** (reflect-cpp):

```cpp
struct AnalogToTensorParams {
    std::string channel_prefix;                // prefix to match AnalogTimeSeries keys
    std::vector<std::string> channel_keys;     // selected DataManager keys (populated from prefix)
};
```

The first-pass UI uses a **hybrid prefix + multi-select** approach:

- `channel_prefix` is a `dynamic_combo` field populated with unique prefixes
  discovered from `AnalogTimeSeries` keys in DataManager (e.g. `"voltage"`,
  `"emg"`).
- When a prefix is selected, `channel_keys` (a `dynamic_combo`
  `std::vector<std::string>` field) is populated with all matching keys and
  rendered as a multi-select `QListWidget` with all items checked by default.
  The user can uncheck individual channels to exclude them.

This is enabled by the `std::vector<T>` support in `ParameterSchema` and
`AutoParamWidget` (see
[ParameterSchema developer docs](../ParameterSchema/index.qmd#stdvectort-auto-detection)).

::: {.callout-note}
**Infrastructure dependency resolved:** `ParameterSchema` now detects
`std::vector<T>` fields at compile time (`is_vector`, `vector_element_type`),
and `AutoParamWidget` renders them as multi-select lists (for
`vector<string>` + `dynamic_combo`) or CSV line edits (for `vector<int>`,
`vector<float>`). All items are checked by default on first population.
The list has a max height of 200px with scrolling for large channel counts.
:::

**Algorithm (implemented):**

1. Validate all channels are non-null, share the same `TimeFrame` (pointer
   identity), and have identical sample counts.
2. Build flat row-major data by iterating each channel via `viewValues()`
   (mmap-safe; avoids the empty-span problem).
3. Return `TensorData::createTimeSeries2D(...)` preserving the shared `TimeFrame`
   and `TimeIndexStorage`. Column names come from `channel_keys` when sizes match,
   otherwise auto-generated as `"ch0"`, `"ch1"`, etc.

### Step 1.2 — TensorToAnalog Container Transform ✓

**Files:**

- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorToAnalog/TensorToAnalog.hpp`
- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorToAnalog/TensorToAnalog.cpp`

**Parameters struct:**

```cpp
struct TensorToAnalogParams {
    std::string output_key_prefix; // prefix for generated AnalogTimeSeries keys
    std::vector<int> columns;      // which columns to extract (empty = all)
};
```

`output_key_prefix` uses a standard `QLineEdit`. `columns` uses a CSV
`QLineEdit` (e.g. `"0, 1, 2"`) via the `std::vector<int>` widget support.
An empty list means "extract all columns".

**Algorithm (implemented):**

1. For each selected column, extract data via `TensorData::getColumn(i)`.
2. When the tensor has `RowType::TimeFrameIndex`, recover time indices via
   `RowDescriptor::timeStoragePtr()->getAllTimeIndices()` and construct the
   `AnalogTimeSeries` with those indices to preserve temporal alignment.
3. Set the source `TimeFrame` on each output series.

::: {.callout-note}
This initial version creates owning copies. Phase 3 replaces this with
zero-copy `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` views.
:::

### Step 1.3 — Registration & Tests ✓

- Both transforms registered via `registerPipelineStepFactoryFor` in
  `RegisteredTransforms.cpp`.
- 12 Catch2 test cases (484 assertions) in
  `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/AnalogToTensor/AnalogToTensor.test.cpp`:
  - Round-trip: N `AnalogTimeSeries` → `AnalogToTensor` → `TensorToAnalog`
    → values match exactly.
  - Single channel, default column name generation.
  - Mismatched TimeFrames → `nullptr` (graceful failure).
  - Mismatched sample counts → `nullptr` (graceful failure).
  - Empty channels vector → `nullptr`.
  - `TimeFrame` and `RowType::TimeFrameIndex` preserved in output.
  - `TensorToAnalog` column selection, out-of-range index rejection.
  - Ordinal tensor extraction.

## Phase 2: Cross-Channel Transforms (CAR, Whitening) {#sec-phase2}

*Depends on Phase 1 (requires multi-channel TensorData).*

### Step 2.1 — Common Average Reference (CAR) Transform ✓

::: {.callout-note}
**Completed.** TensorCAR is implemented, registered, and tested.
See [developer docs](../transforms_v2/tensor_car.qmd) for full API reference.
:::

**New files:**

- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorCAR/TensorCAR.hpp`
- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorCAR/TensorCAR.cpp`

**Transform type:** Container (`TensorData` → `TensorData`).

**Parameters struct:**

```cpp
struct TensorCARParams {
    CARMethod method = CARMethod::Median; // Mean or Median
    std::vector<int> exclude_channels;    // channels excluded from reference
    bool use_gpu = false;
};
```

**Algorithm (CPU via Armadillo):**

1. `auto t = tensor.toLibTorch()` — convert from Armadillo storage.
2. If `use_gpu`: `t = DeviceManager::instance().toDevice(t)` — move to GPU.
3. Compute reference:
   - Mean: `auto ref = torch::mean(t, /*dim=*/1).values`
   - Median: `auto ref = torch::median(t, /*dim=*/1).values`
4. Subtract: `t = t - ref.unsqueeze(1)`.
5. Return `TensorData` wrapping the result `LibTorchTensorStorage`.

The same code runs on CPU and GPU — the only difference is the device
placement in step 2. See @sec-compute-backend for rationale.

**Pattern reference:** `TensorPCA` (container transform, `TensorData` → `TensorData`).

### Step 2.2 — Spatial Whitening Transform

**New files:**

- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorWhitening/TensorWhitening.hpp`
- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorWhitening/TensorWhitening.cpp`

**Parameters struct:**

```cpp
struct TensorWhiteningParams {
    WhiteningMethod method = WhiteningMethod::ZCA; // ZCA, PCA, or Cholesky
    float regularization = 1e-5f;
    bool use_gpu = false;
};
```

**Algorithm (unified LibTorch path):**

1. `auto t = tensor.toLibTorch()` — convert from Armadillo storage.
2. If `use_gpu`: `t = DeviceManager::instance().toDevice(t)`.
3. Compute covariance: $C = \frac{1}{n-1} X^T X$ via `torch::mm(t.t(), t) / (n - 1)`.
4. Eigendecomposition: `auto [eigval, eigvec] = torch::linalg::eigh(cov)`.
5. Whitening matrix (ZCA):
   $$W = V \cdot \mathrm{diag}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i + \epsilon}}\right) \cdot V^T$$
   ```cpp
   auto D_inv = torch::diag(1.0f / torch::sqrt(eigval + reg));
   auto W = torch::mm(torch::mm(eigvec, D_inv), eigvec.t());
   ```
6. Apply: `auto result = torch::mm(t, W)`.
7. Return `TensorData` wrapping the result `LibTorchTensorStorage`.

### Step 2.3 — Per-Channel IIR Filter on TensorData

**New files:**

- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorIIRFilter/TensorIIRFilter.hpp`
- `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorIIRFilter/TensorIIRFilter.cpp`

**Transform type:** Container (`TensorData` → `TensorData`).

**Parameters struct:**

```cpp
struct TensorIIRFilterParams {
    FilterSpecification filter_spec; // reuses existing FilterSpecification
};
```

This applies the same IIR filter independently to every column (channel) of the
tensor. It is the multi-channel counterpart to the existing single-channel
`AnalogFilterOperation`.

**Algorithm (column-at-a-time on Armadillo storage):**

1. Ensure the tensor has Armadillo storage: `tensor.toArmadillo()`.
2. Create the filter from the specification: `auto filter = filter_spec.createFilter()`.
3. For each column $c$ in $[0, N_{\text{channels}})$:
   a. Get contiguous column pointer: `float* col = mat.colptr(c)`.
   b. Apply the IIR filter in-place on the contiguous span.
   c. Reset filter state for the next channel.
4. Return `TensorData` wrapping the filtered matrix.

Because Armadillo uses column-major layout, each column is a contiguous block of
`num_rows` floats. The inner filter loop is identical to the existing
single-channel `AnalogTimeSeries` filter — same tight span, same cache
utilization, same speed. No virtual dispatch, no block boundary concerns.

::: {.callout-tip}
**Why not filter through the `AnalogTimeSeries` interface?**

If multi-channel data is stored in a block cache (Phase 4), individual
`AnalogTimeSeries` channels see interleaved or block-strided data. Running an
IIR filter through per-element virtual dispatch on that layout would be orders
of magnitude slower than operating on contiguous columns. The TensorData path
ensures the data is materialized into contiguous columns *before* the filter
runs. See @sec-transform-responsibility.
:::

**Future extension:** For FIR filters (or IIR filters expressed as cascaded
second-order sections), LibTorch's `torch::nn::functional::conv1d` can process
all channels in a single batched call, parallelizing across channels.

### Step 2.4 — Tests

- **CAR:** Verify that after CAR, the row-wise mean (or median) is approximately
  zero: $\lVert \bar{x}_{\text{row}} \rVert < \epsilon$.
- **Whitening:** Verify the covariance of the result approximates the identity
  matrix: $\lVert \mathrm{cov}(X_{\text{white}}) - I \rVert_F < \epsilon$.
- **IIR Filter:** Verify output matches single-channel `filter_analog()` applied
  independently to each column. Test with Butterworth lowpass on known sinusoidal
  input.
- **CPU vs GPU:** Verify results match within floating-point tolerance (if CUDA
  is available at test time). Since both paths execute the same LibTorch code,
  divergence should be limited to GPU floating-point non-determinism.

## Phase 3: Zero-Copy TensorData Column Views {#sec-phase3}

*Depends on Phase 1. Enables zero-copy channel access.*

This phase eliminates the data copy in `TensorToAnalog` by creating an
`AnalogTimeSeries` storage backend that directly references a `TensorData` column.

### Step 3.1 — TensorColumnAnalogStorage Backend

**New file:**
`src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/TensorColumnAnalogStorage.hpp`

**Members:**

```cpp
std::shared_ptr<TensorData const> _tensor; // shared ownership of source
std::size_t _column_index;
```

**Key insight:** Armadillo uses column-major layout. Each column of an `arma::fmat`
is contiguous in memory. This means `fmat.colptr(col_index)` gives a `float*` to a
contiguous block of `num_rows` floats — exactly what `AnalogTimeSeries` needs for
span-based access.

**CRTP implementation:**

| Method | Implementation |
|--------|---------------|
| `getValueAtImpl(i)` | `_tensor->at({i, _column_index})` |
| `sizeImpl()` | `_tensor->numRows()` |
| `isContiguousImpl()` | `true` if Armadillo backend (column-major) |
| `tryGetCacheImpl()` | Return `{colptr, num_rows, true}` if Armadillo |
| `getSpanRangeImpl(s, e)` | Return `span{colptr + s, e - s}` if Armadillo |

: CRTP method implementations for TensorColumnAnalogStorage {.striped}

This is a significant improvement over the current mmap storage, where
`getSpanRangeImpl()` always returns an empty span.

### Step 3.2 — Factory Method

Add to `AnalogTimeSeries`:

```cpp
static std::shared_ptr<AnalogTimeSeries> createFromTensorColumn(
    std::shared_ptr<TensorData const> tensor,
    std::size_t column_index,
    std::shared_ptr<TimeIndexStorage> time_storage);
```

### Step 3.3 — Update TensorToAnalog

Modify the Phase 1 `TensorToAnalog` transform to use `createFromTensorColumn`
instead of copying column data. Result: applying CAR → `TensorToAnalog` produces
views that share the filtered `TensorData` memory.

### Step 3.4 — Tests

- **Zero-copy verification:** Confirm that the `AnalogTimeSeries` view and the
  source `TensorData` share the same memory (pointer comparison).
- **Span access:** Verify `getDataInTimeFrameIndexRange` returns a non-empty span
  (unlike current mmap behavior).
- **DataViewer compatibility:** Ensure the new backend works with the existing
  `DataViewer_Widget` rendering pipeline.

## DataViewer Integration {#sec-dataviewer-integration}

`DataViewer_Widget` is the primary consumer of multi-channel `AnalogTimeSeries`
data. It groups channels by naming convention (e.g. `voltage_1`, `voltage_2`, …
under a `voltage` group) and plots them as stacked 1D temporal traces. Today
this works well for in-memory data but is slow for memory-mapped channels because
each channel independently reads the same file pages.

A critical design goal of this plan is that **DataViewer_Widget does not need to
change.** The storage abstraction handles multi-channel efficiency transparently.

### In-memory path (Phase 3)

When data fits in memory, the loading path creates one `TensorData` (Armadillo)
and $N$ `TensorColumnAnalogStorage`-backed `AnalogTimeSeries` objects. Each one
has a contiguous column span. When DataViewer calls
`getDataInTimeFrameIndexRange()`, it receives a real `std::span<float const>`
pointing into the Armadillo matrix column. This is exactly as fast as the
current `VectorAnalogDataStorage` path. DataViewer sees no difference.

### Block-cached mmap path (Phase 4)

When data is too large for memory, the loading path creates one
`SharedMmapBlockCache` and $N$ `BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage`-backed
`AnalogTimeSeries` objects. All $N$ channels share the same cache via
`std::shared_ptr`. When DataViewer renders the visible time window for a group:

```
DataViewer renders voltage_0 at t=[5000, 6000):
  → BlockCachedMmap asks cache for samples 5000–6000
  → Cache miss → loadBlock() decodes ALL N channels for that block
  → Returns data for channel 0

DataViewer renders voltage_1 at t=[5000, 6000):
  → BlockCachedMmap asks cache for samples 5000–6000
  → Cache HIT → returns channel 1 data instantly

... (channels 2 through N−1 all hit cache)

User scrolls to t=[6500, 7500):
  → voltage_0 triggers one new block load
  → voltage_1 through voltage_{N−1} all hit cache again
```

The shared block cache is the mechanism that makes this fast. The first channel
pays the decode cost; the remaining $N - 1$ are free lookups. DataViewer does
not orchestrate this — it happens automatically because all `AnalogTimeSeries`
objects reference the same `SharedMmapBlockCache`.

For a typical DataViewer render (1000–2000 visible samples per channel), even
element-by-element access into a decoded float32 cache block is fast —
microseconds per channel. The expensive part today is per-element stride
calculation + `memcpy` + int16-to-float conversion from raw mmap, repeated
independently per channel with no shared state. The block cache eliminates
that entirely: type conversion happens once at block load, and subsequent
access is plain float array indexing.

### Where group awareness matters: loading time

The one place where multi-channel awareness is needed at the group level is
**data import**, not viewing. When the user loads a multi-channel binary file,
the loading code decides:

| Condition | Strategy | Resulting AnalogTimeSeries backend |
|-----------|----------|------------------------------------|
| File fits in memory | Materialize into `TensorData` (Armadillo) | `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` (contiguous spans) |
| File too large | Create `SharedMmapBlockCache` | `BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage` (cached lookups) |

: Loading strategy selection at import time {.striped}

This decision is made once at import time by the `DataImport_Widget` or loading
command. After that, DataViewer just sees `AnalogTimeSeries` objects registered
under the usual naming convention and plots them with its existing code.

## Tensor-Backed Binary Loading {#sec-tensor-loading}

Phases 1–3 define `TensorColumnAnalogStorage` and
`BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage`, but those backends are useless if the loading
path never creates them. Today the binary loader
([Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp](../../../src/IO/formats/Binary/analogtimeseries/Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp))
returns `vector<shared_ptr<AnalogTimeSeries>>` — each channel is an independent
object with either `VectorAnalogDataStorage` (in-memory) or
`MemoryMappedAnalogDataStorage` (mmap, per-channel stride). No `TensorData` is
created and no channels share state.

This section adds a tensor-aware loading strategy to `src/IO/formats/Binary/`
so that multi-channel data is loaded with the correct backend from the start,
rather than requiring a post-hoc `AnalogToTensor` transform.

### Current loader architecture

The existing binary analog loading flow is:

```
BinaryAnalogImport_Widget  (UI: collects parameters)
        │
        ▼  signal: loadBinaryAnalogRequested(BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions)
AnalogImport_Widget::_handleBinaryLoadRequested()
        │
        ▼  calls free function ::load(opts)
Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp
        │
        ├─ use_memory_mapped=true:
        │    for each channel:
        │      MmapStorageConfig { offset=channel, stride=N_ch }
        │      → MemoryMappedAnalogDataStorage (independent per channel)
        │
        └─ use_memory_mapped=false:
             readBinaryFileMultiChannel<int16_t>()
             → de-interleave into N vectors
             → N independent VectorAnalogDataStorage objects
```

Both paths produce independent `AnalogTimeSeries` objects. The in-memory path
is additionally **hardcoded to int16_t** and ignores the `binary_data_type`
parameter (a bug the mmap path does not have).

### New loading mode: `use_tensor_backed`

Add a new boolean option to `BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions`:

```cpp
struct BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions {
    // ... existing fields ...
    std::optional<bool> use_tensor_backed;  // default false (backward-compatible)
};
```

When `use_tensor_backed=true` *and* `num_channels > 1`, the loader creates
tensor-backed storage instead of independent per-channel objects.

#### In-memory tensor-backed path

When `use_tensor_backed=true` and `use_memory_mapped=false`:

```
::load(opts)  [Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp]
        │
        ▼
1. Read entire interleaved file via readBinaryFileMultiChannel<T>()
   (fix: dispatch on binary_data_type instead of hardcoding int16_t)
        │
        ▼
2. Allocate arma::fmat(num_samples, num_channels)
   Copy de-interleaved channels into columns with scale/offset
        │
        ▼
3. Create TensorData::createTimeSeries2D(mat, col_names, time_storage)
        │
        ▼
4. For each column c in [0, num_channels):
     AnalogTimeSeries::createFromTensorColumn(tensor, c, time_storage)
        │
        ▼
5. Return { tensor_data, vector<AnalogTimeSeries> }
```

The `TensorData` owns the memory. Each `AnalogTimeSeries` is a zero-copy
`TensorColumnAnalogStorage` view into a column. DataViewer sees contiguous
spans — identical to the current `VectorAnalogDataStorage` path.

#### Memory-mapped tensor-backed path

When `use_tensor_backed=true` and `use_memory_mapped=true`:

```
::load(opts)  [Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp]
        │
        ▼
1. Create SharedMmapBlockCache from file with multi-channel config:
     { file_path, header_size, num_channels, data_type,
       scale_factor, offset_value, block_size_samples }
        │
        ▼
2. For each channel c in [0, num_channels):
     AnalogTimeSeries::createBlockCachedMmap(cache, c, time_vector)
        │
        ▼
3. Return vector<AnalogTimeSeries>
   (all sharing the same SharedMmapBlockCache via shared_ptr)
```

All $N$ channels share the same `SharedMmapBlockCache`. When DataViewer
renders one channel's time window, all $N$ channels' data for that window
is decoded into the cache. Subsequent channels hit the cache. See
@sec-dataviewer-integration for the access pattern walkthrough.

### Return type change

The current `::load()` returns `vector<shared_ptr<AnalogTimeSeries>>`. The
tensor-backed in-memory path also produces a `TensorData` that must be
registered in DataManager (it is the memory owner; the `AnalogTimeSeries`
views point into it). Two approaches:

**Option A — Expanded return type:**

```cpp
struct BinaryAnalogLoadResult {
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<AnalogTimeSeries>> channels;
    std::shared_ptr<TensorData> tensor;  // non-null when tensor-backed
};
```

The caller (`AnalogImport_Widget::_handleBinaryLoadRequested`) registers the
`TensorData` in DataManager under `"{key}_tensor"` and each channel under
`"{key}_ch{i}"`. This mirrors the existing multi-channel naming pattern.

**Option B — Return through `BatchLoadResult`:**

Have `BinaryFormatLoader::loadAnalogBatch()` return both the `TensorData` and
the per-channel `AnalogTimeSeries` in the `BatchLoadResult::results` vector
(which already holds a `LoadedDataVariant` that includes
`shared_ptr<TensorData>`). This keeps the `IFormatLoader` interface unchanged.

Option A is preferred because it makes the tensor ownership explicit at the
call site and avoids overloading `BatchLoadResult` with mixed types.

### Changes to `AnalogImport_Widget`

`_handleBinaryLoadRequested()` gains a branch for the tensor-backed case:

```cpp
void AnalogImport_Widget::_handleBinaryLoadRequested(BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions options) {
    auto const analog_key = ui->data_name_text->text().toStdString();
    // ... existing validation ...

    auto result = load(options);

    if (result.tensor) {
        // Tensor-backed path: register the TensorData first (it owns the memory)
        _data_manager->setData<TensorData>(
            analog_key + "_tensor", result.tensor, TimeKey("time"));

        // Register per-channel views
        for (size_t i = 0; i < result.channels.size(); ++i) {
            std::string channel_key = analog_key + "_ch" + std::to_string(i);
            _data_manager->setData<AnalogTimeSeries>(
                channel_key, result.channels[i], TimeKey("time"));
        }
    } else {
        // Existing independent-channel path (unchanged)
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Changes to `BinaryFormatLoader`

`loadAnalogBatch()` must handle the new return type and include the
`TensorData` in the `BatchLoadResult` when present:

```cpp
BatchLoadResult BinaryFormatLoader::loadAnalogBatch(...) {
    auto result = ::load(opts);
    std::vector<LoadResult> load_results;

    // If a TensorData was created, include it first
    if (result.tensor) {
        LoadResult tensor_lr(std::move(result.tensor));
        tensor_lr.name = "tensor";
        load_results.push_back(std::move(tensor_lr));
    }

    // Per-channel AnalogTimeSeries
    for (size_t i = 0; i < result.channels.size(); ++i) {
        LoadResult lr(std::move(result.channels[i]));
        lr.name = std::to_string(i);
        load_results.push_back(std::move(lr));
    }

    return BatchLoadResult::fromVector(std::move(load_results));
}
```

This means JSON-based loading (where `DataManagerIO` calls
`BinaryFormatLoader::loadBatch()` without a widget) also benefits from
tensor-backed storage.

### Bug fix: in-memory data type dispatch

The current in-memory path is hardcoded to `int16_t`:

```cpp
auto data = Loader::readBinaryFileMultiChannel<int16_t>(binary_loader_opts);
```

This should dispatch on `binary_data_type` to match the mmap path. The
tensor-backed path fixes this by construction (type conversion happens during
the copy into `arma::fmat`), but the legacy independent-channel path should
also be fixed:

```cpp
if (opts.getBinaryDataType() == "float32") {
    data = Loader::readBinaryFileMultiChannel<float>(binary_loader_opts);
} else if (opts.getBinaryDataType() == "int16") {
    data = Loader::readBinaryFileMultiChannel<int16_t>(binary_loader_opts);
}
// ... etc
```

### UI changes

`BinaryAnalogImport_Widget` needs a checkbox for "Tensor-backed (shared
storage)" that maps to `use_tensor_backed`. The checkbox is enabled only
when `num_channels > 1`. When checked, the "Memory mapped" checkbox
switches between the in-memory tensor path and the block-cached mmap path.

### JSON loading support

For headless / pipeline loading via JSON config files, the
`BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions` struct already uses reflect-cpp, so adding
`use_tensor_backed` makes it available in JSON configs immediately:

```json
{
    "data_type": "Analog",
    "format": "binary",
    "name": "voltage",
    "filepath": "recording.bin",
    "num_channels": 32,
    "binary_data_type": "int16",
    "scale_factor": 0.195,
    "use_tensor_backed": true,
    "use_memory_mapped": false
}
```

This creates a `TensorData` named `"voltage_tensor"` and 32
`AnalogTimeSeries` named `"voltage_ch0"` through `"voltage_ch31"`, each
backed by `TensorColumnAnalogStorage`.

### Dependency ordering

This section depends on:

- **Phase 3** (`TensorColumnAnalogStorage`) for the in-memory tensor-backed path
- **Phase 4** (`SharedMmapBlockCache`, `BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage`) for the
  mmap tensor-backed path

The in-memory tensor-backed path can ship as soon as Phase 3 is complete.
The mmap tensor-backed path ships with Phase 4. The `use_tensor_backed`
option and return type change can land with Phase 3; the mmap branch is
gated on `SharedMmapBlockCache` availability.

### Tests

- **In-memory tensor-backed:** Load a multi-channel binary file with
  `use_tensor_backed=true`. Verify `TensorData` column count matches
  `num_channels`. Verify each `AnalogTimeSeries` view shares memory with
  the `TensorData` (pointer comparison on `getSpanRangeImpl`). Verify values
  match the legacy independent-channel path.
- **Mmap tensor-backed:** Load with `use_tensor_backed=true` and
  `use_memory_mapped=true`. Verify all channels share the same
  `SharedMmapBlockCache` instance. Verify values match element-by-element
  against the legacy per-channel mmap path.
- **Data type dispatch fix:** Load float32 binary files with the legacy
  in-memory path. Verify values are correct (currently broken for non-int16).
- **JSON round-trip:** Parse a `BinaryAnalogLoaderOptions` JSON with
  `use_tensor_backed=true`, verify it round-trips through reflect-cpp.

## Phase 4: Shared Block-Cached Mmap Storage {#sec-phase4}

*Independent of Phases 1–3. Addresses the I/O efficiency problem.*

This phase introduces a file-level block cache that ensures when one channel reads
a time range, all other channels' data in that range is decoded and cached
simultaneously.

::: {.callout-important}
**The block cache is for I/O and viewing, not hot-loop compute.**

The cache provides efficient access for scrolling, plotting, and loading data
into `TensorData`. It does *not* need to provide contiguous per-channel spans
for filter loops. Heavy per-channel compute (IIR filtering, spectral analysis)
happens after materializing data into `TensorData` where Armadillo column-major
layout guarantees contiguous columns. See @sec-transform-responsibility.
:::

### Step 4.1 — SharedMmapBlockCache

**New files:**

- `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/SharedMmapBlockCache.hpp`
- `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/SharedMmapBlockCache.cpp`

**Design:** A shared resource that memory-maps an interleaved binary file and
maintains a circular buffer of decoded float blocks.

**Members:**

- `void* _mapped_data` — OS-level mmap of the entire file.
- Circular buffer of $N$ decoded blocks ($N$ configurable, default 16).
- Each block: contiguous `std::vector<float>` of size
  `block_size_samples × num_channels` (row-major: time varies fastest within a
  channel's stride).
- Block time range tracking: `[start_sample, end_sample)` per block.
- Shared `MmapStorageConfig` (file path, header, num_channels, data_type, scale).

**Key methods:**

| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `getBlock(time_sample)` | Returns view of block containing that sample; loads from mmap if not cached |
| `loadBlock(start_sample)` | Bulk-decodes `block_size × num_channels` elements from mmap into float block |
| `getChannelValue(time, ch)` | O(1) lookup within cached block |
| `getChannelSpan(start, end, ch)` | Zero-copy span if block-aligned |

: SharedMmapBlockCache public API {.striped}

#### Bulk Type Conversion in `loadBlock` {#sec-bulk-decode}

ADC data is typically stored as int16. The current per-element mmap storage
performs `memcpy` + `static_cast<float>` + scale + offset for every single
sample. In the block cache, type conversion happens **once per block load**
for all channels simultaneously.

LibTorch provides an efficient bulk conversion path:

```cpp
// Interpret raw mmap bytes as int16 tensor (zero-copy via from_blob)
auto raw = torch::from_blob(
    mmap_ptr + byte_offset,
    {block_samples, num_channels},
    torch::kInt16);

// Fused convert + scale + offset (single vectorized pass)
auto decoded = raw.to(torch::kFloat32) * scale_factor + offset_value;
```

This is a single fused kernel that benefits from SIMD on CPU (and is trivially
GPU-portable if on-device decode is ever desired). After decode, the block
contains native float32 data ready for computation or span access.

Alternatively, a plain C++ loop with explicit SIMD intrinsics or
`std::transform` can be used if avoiding the LibTorch dependency at the
storage layer is preferred. The key design point is: **type conversion and
scaling happen exactly once, at block load time.** All downstream access
operates on float32.

**Eviction policy:** FIFO circular buffer, optimized for sequential temporal
access. Random-access workloads may benefit from LRU, but FIFO is simpler and
sufficient for the dominant scrolling/playback pattern.

**Thread safety:** Reader-writer lock on the block table. Multiple readers are
allowed; a single writer for block loads. This supports the common pattern of
multiple DataViewer widgets reading different channels simultaneously.

### Step 4.2 — BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage

**New file:**
`src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage.hpp`

A new `AnalogTimeSeries` storage backend that delegates to `SharedMmapBlockCache`.

**Members:**

- `std::shared_ptr<SharedMmapBlockCache> _cache`
- `std::size_t _channel_index`

**CRTP implementation:**

| Method | Implementation |
|--------|---------------|
| `getValueAtImpl(i)` | `_cache->getChannelValue(i, _channel_index)` — O(1) in cached block |
| `getSpanRangeImpl(s, e)` | Span from cache block if range fits in one block; temp buffer for cross-block |
| `tryGetCacheImpl()` | Cache pointer for the current block if known |
| `isContiguousImpl()` | `false` (data is interleaved in cache blocks) |

: BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage CRTP methods {.striped}

**Benefit:** When 32 channels read sample range $[1000, 2000)$, the first
channel triggers a block load decoding all 32 channels. The remaining 31 channels
hit the cache. This reduces I/O by a factor of $\sim N_{\text{channels}}$.

### Step 4.3 — MmapTensorStorage for TensorData

**New file:** `src/DataObjects/Tensors/storage/MmapTensorStorage.hpp`

A `TensorData` storage backend backed by `SharedMmapBlockCache`. This enables
loading interleaved binary files directly as `TensorData` with shape
`[time, channels]`.

Combined with Phase 3's `TensorColumnAnalogStorage`, this provides the full
pipeline: binary file → block-cached TensorData → zero-copy AnalogTimeSeries
views per channel.

### Step 4.4 — Configuration Changes

Extend `MmapStorageConfig` with multi-channel awareness:

```cpp
struct MmapStorageConfig {
    // ... existing fields ...
    std::size_t num_channels = 1;         // total channels in file
    std::size_t block_size_samples = 4096;// samples per cache block
    std::size_t num_cache_blocks = 16;    // blocks in circular buffer
};
```

### Step 4.5 — Factory Methods

- `AnalogTimeSeries::createBlockCachedMmap(shared_ptr<SharedMmapBlockCache>,
  channel_index, time_vector)`
- `TensorData::createFromMmap(MmapStorageConfig, time_vector)` — creates
  multi-channel tensor directly from interleaved binary.
- Group creation helper: create $N$ `AnalogTimeSeries` from one
  `SharedMmapBlockCache`.

### Step 4.6 — Tests & Benchmarks

- **Correctness:** Verify values match element-by-element against current
  `MemoryMappedAnalogDataStorage`.
- **Block efficiency:** Verify block loads =
  $\lceil \text{range} / \text{block\_size} \rceil$, not
  $\text{range} \times N_{\text{channels}}$.
- **Cross-block reads:** Verify correct stitching when a requested range spans
  two blocks.
- **Benchmark:** Compare block-cached vs current element-by-element for
  384-channel data at various block sizes.

### Cache Sizing Guidance

The cache memory footprint is:

$$M = N_{\text{blocks}} \times B_{\text{samples}} \times N_{\text{channels}} \times 4 \;\text{bytes}$$

| Channels | Block Size | Num Blocks | Memory |
|----------|-----------|------------|--------|
| 32 | 4096 | 16 | 8 MB |
| 64 | 4096 | 16 | 16 MB |
| 384 | 4096 | 16 | 96 MB |
| 384 | 4096 | 8 | 48 MB |
| 384 | 2048 | 8 | 24 MB |

: Cache memory footprint for common configurations {.striped}

For Neuropixels-scale recordings (384 channels), 8 blocks of 4096 samples
(~48 MB) provides a reasonable trade-off between memory and cache hit rate
for typical scrolling speeds.

## Phase 5: GPU-Accelerated Processing {#sec-phase5}

*Depends on Phase 2. Phase 4 is optional but improves large-dataset throughput.*

Because Phase 2 already uses LibTorch as the compute backend, GPU acceleration
is a matter of device placement rather than a new code path. This phase focuses
on **large-dataset batching** and **benchmarking**.

### Step 5.1 — Temporal Block Processing for Large Datasets

When a dataset exceeds GPU memory (or is too large to materialize fully on CPU),
process it in temporal blocks:

1. Iterate over time in chunks of $B$ samples (matching Phase 4 cache block size
   or configurable independently).
2. For each chunk: extract `TensorData` slice → `toLibTorch()` →
   `DeviceManager::instance().toDevice()` → compute → download result.
3. Concatenate results.

This reuses the existing Phase 2 transform code unmodified — the batching
logic wraps the transform rather than modifying it.

### Step 5.2 — Benchmarks

- GPU vs CPU CAR on 384 channels × 30,000 samples.
- GPU vs CPU whitening.
- Block upload/download overhead measurement.
- Determine the crossover point where GPU becomes faster than CPU
  (expected: GPU wins for large channel counts and/or long time windows;
  CPU wins for small problems where transfer overhead dominates).

## Verification Summary

| Phase | Test Command | Acceptance Criteria | Status |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|--------|
| ~~0~~ | `ctest -R "test_data_manager_all"` | Values match element-by-element path; result is contiguous | **Done** |
| 1 | `ctest -R "AnalogToTensor\|TensorToAnalog"` | Round-trip fidelity, error on mismatched timeframes | |
| 2 | `ctest -R "TensorCAR\|TensorWhitening\|TensorIIRFilter"` | Row-wise mean ≈ 0 (CAR); cov ≈ I (whitening); IIR matches single-channel | |
| 3 | `ctest -R "TensorColumnAnalog"` | Zero-copy pointer match; non-empty span from `getDataInTimeFrameIndexRange` | |
| 3 | `ctest -R "BinaryTensorBacked"` | TensorData created; views share memory; values match legacy path | |
| 4 | `ctest -R "SharedMmapBlockCache"` | Correct values; block load count = ⌈range/block_size⌉ | |
| 5 | Benchmark suite | GPU matches CPU within tolerance; speedup measured | |

: Per-phase verification {.striped}

**Integration test (manual):** Load 32-channel binary with `use_tensor_backed=true`
→ verify `TensorData` + 32 `AnalogTimeSeries` views appear in DataManager → apply
CAR transform on the `TensorData` → `TensorToAnalog` → view in DataViewer. Verify
memory usage stays bounded (Phase 3 zero-copy, Phase 4 block cache bounded).

## File Inventory

### Existing files to modify

| File | Phase | Change |
|------|-------|--------|
| ~~`src/DataObjects/Tensors/TensorData.cpp`~~ | ~~0~~ | ~~Armadillo fast path in `toLibTorch()`~~ **Done** |
| `src/TransformsV2/core/RegisteredTransforms.cpp` | 1, 2 | Register new transforms |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/Analog_Time_Series.hpp` | 3, 4 | Add factory methods |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/AnalogDataStorage.hpp` | 3, 4 | Type-erasure for new backends |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/MmapAnalogConfig.hpp` | 4 | Add multi-channel fields |
| `src/DataObjects/Tensors/TensorData.hpp` | 4 | Add `createFromMmap` factory |
| `src/DataObjects/Tensors/storage/TensorStorageWrapper.hpp` | 4 | Register new backend |
| `src/IO/formats/Binary/analogtimeseries/Analog_Time_Series_Binary.hpp` | 3 | Add `use_tensor_backed` option, `BinaryAnalogLoadResult` return type |
| `src/IO/formats/Binary/analogtimeseries/Analog_Time_Series_Binary.cpp` | 3, 4 | Tensor-backed loading paths, data type dispatch fix |
| `src/IO/formats/Binary/BinaryFormatLoader.cpp` | 3 | Handle `BinaryAnalogLoadResult` in `loadAnalogBatch()` |
| `src/WhiskerToolbox/DataImport_Widget/AnalogTimeSeries/AnalogImport_Widget.cpp` | 3 | Register `TensorData` + per-channel views in DataManager |
| `src/WhiskerToolbox/DataImport_Widget/AnalogTimeSeries/Binary/BinaryAnalogImport_Widget.cpp` | 3 | Add "Tensor-backed" checkbox |

: Existing files requiring modification {.striped}

### New files to create

| File | Phase |
|------|-------|
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/AnalogToTensor/AnalogToTensor.hpp` | 1 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/AnalogToTensor/AnalogToTensor.cpp` | 1 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorToAnalog/TensorToAnalog.hpp` | 1 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorToAnalog/TensorToAnalog.cpp` | 1 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorCAR/TensorCAR.hpp` | 2 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorCAR/TensorCAR.cpp` | 2 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorWhitening/TensorWhitening.hpp` | 2 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorWhitening/TensorWhitening.cpp` | 2 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorIIRFilter/TensorIIRFilter.hpp` | 2 |
| `src/TransformsV2/algorithms/TensorIIRFilter/TensorIIRFilter.cpp` | 2 |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/TensorColumnAnalogStorage.hpp` | 3 |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/SharedMmapBlockCache.hpp` | 4 |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/SharedMmapBlockCache.cpp` | 4 |
| `src/DataObjects/AnalogTimeSeries/storage/BlockCachedMmapAnalogStorage.hpp` | 4 |
| `src/DataObjects/Tensors/storage/MmapTensorStorage.hpp` | 4 |

: New files to create {.striped}

## Excluded Scope

The following are explicitly out of scope for this plan:

- **UI widgets** for multi-channel configuration or channel group management.
- **Spectral transforms** (FFT, spectrogram) — future work using the same
  TensorData infrastructure built here.
- **`madvise` / `PrefetchVirtualMemory` hints** for mmap prefetching — a
  low-hanging optimization that can be added to `SharedMmapBlockCache` later.
- **Distributed / multi-GPU compute.**
